1: The cry to the Shepherd of Israel enthroned above the cherubim reaches its fulfillment in the one who came as the Good Shepherd (John 10:11) — the very YHWH who led Joseph like a flock now takes on flesh to seek the lost sheep of Israel. The throne above the cherubim that the psalmist addresses is the one from which the Son descends (John 3:13; Hebrews 1:3).
2: The summons to stir up your might before Ephraim, Benjamin, and Manasseh — tribes of the Rachel-Joseph line — is a plea for the powerful showing-up of God. The disciples who witnessed Christ transfigured and resurrected saw precisely this: the divine might displayed not in military power but in the power of an indestructible life (Hebrews 7:16).
3: The refrain — shuvennu Elohim haer panekha venivvashea — "Restore us, O God; make your face shine, so that we may be saved" — is the Aaronic blessing (Numbers 6:25-26) transformed into desperate petition. In Christ, the shining face of God is given permanently: "God... has shone in our hearts to give the light of the knowledge of God's glory in the face of Jesus Christ" (2 Corinthians 4:6). The shining that the psalmist begs for is the very face the disciples beheld (John 1:14).
4: "How long, O LORD God of hosts, will you be angry with your people's prayers?" — the smoke of divine anger that seems to consume prayer itself. Christ on the cross cries "My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?" (Matthew 27:46), identifying fully with the community whose prayers seem to go nowhere; and then, risen, he opens the way to the Father so that prayers are no longer met with smoke but with the Spirit's intercession (Romans 8:26-27).
5: "You have fed them with the bread of tears and given them tears to drink in full measure" — the divine feeding with sorrow. Christ, the Bread of Life (John 6:35), entered into this very experience: "Man of sorrows and acquainted with grief" (Isaiah 53:3). The one who feeds with bread of tears is the same one who becomes the bread that ends the tears (Revelation 21:4: "He will wipe away every tear").
6: "You make us an object of contention for our neighbors, and our enemies laugh among themselves" — the scorn of surrounding nations. Jesus bore this scorn explicitly: he was mocked by Herod's soldiers (Luke 23:11), the crowd wagged their heads (Matthew 27:39), and the rulers "scoffed" (Luke 23:35). The shame borne by Israel that the psalmist laments is the shame Christ absorbed as Israel's representative head.
7: The refrain recurs: "Restore us, O God of hosts; make your face shine, so that we may be saved." The three-fold repetition of this refrain across the psalm (vv3, 7, 19) mirrors the threefold divine name escalation: first "God" (Elohim), then "God of hosts" (Elohei Tsevat), finally "LORD God of hosts" (YHWH Elohei Tsevat) — an intensification that finds its fullest expression in Christ who is "Emmanuel, God with us" (Matthew 1:23), the complete shining of the divine face.
8: "You brought a vine out of Egypt; you drove out the nations and planted it" — the great vine-transplant of the Exodus. Jesus said "I am the true vine" (John 15:1), explicitly claiming to be what Israel the historic vine failed to be. The vine-from-Egypt that should have filled the land is now the true vine from which the branches draw life — the Exodus-vine history is not abandoned but fulfilled and surpassed.
9: "You cleared the ground for it; it took deep root and filled the land" — the flourishing of the planted vine. The true vine planted in the land, Jesus of Nazareth, took root in the soil of Jewish history (Matthew 1:1-17), bore fruit through death and resurrection, and now fills the earth as his people spread to every nation (Acts 1:8; Revelation 7:9). The geographical filling of the land points toward the eschatological filling of the earth.
10: "The mountains were covered with its shade, the mighty cedars with its branches" — the vine's cosmic reach. The mustard seed parable (Matthew 13:31-32) uses the same image: a tiny plant that grows until "the birds of the air come and perch in its branches." Christ's kingdom begins as the most modest of plantings in Galilee and expands to fill the earth; the vine of Psalms 80:10 is the mustard-tree of the Gospels.
11: "It sent out its branches to the sea, and its shoots to the River" — the vine spanning from Mediterranean to Euphrates, the full extent of the Davidic promise (Genesis 15:18; 1 Kings 4:21). Ephesians 1:10 speaks of God's plan to bring all things together under Christ — the vine whose branches reach from sea to river becomes the cosmic Christ who fills all in all (Ephesians 1:23).
12: "Why then have you broken down its walls, so that all who pass along the way pluck its fruit?" — the ruined vineyard, open to plunder. Isaiah 5:1-7 uses the same image of the unprotected vineyard to speak of divine judgment. Christ quotes the parable of the wicked tenants (Matthew 21:33-44) over this same vineyard — the story of Israel's failure is one Jesus knows from the inside, as both the rejected cornerstone and the true Son sent last.
13: "A boar from the forest ravages it, and all that move in the field feed on it" — the wild beast tearing apart the vine. Christ came to destroy the works of the devil (1 John 3:8), to bind the strong man and plunder his house (Matthew 12:29). The vine that was ravaged by the enemy is defended by the Lion of Judah (Revelation 5:5).
14: "Turn again, O God of hosts! Look down from heaven, and see; have regard for this vine" — the prayer that God would look at the vine. The Incarnation is the divine response to this prayer: God does not merely look down from heaven but descends to the vine in the person of his Son. "The LORD looked down from his holy height" (Psalms 102:19) finds its ultimate expression in the One who stooped from highest heaven to the manger (Philippians 2:6-7).
15: "The stock that your right hand planted, and the son that you made strong for yourself" — the vine personalized as a son. Here is the Christological hinge: the vine is simultaneously a son. Jesus is the True Vine (John 15:1) and the Son of God — the personification of Israel-vine in a single person. "This is my beloved Son" (Matthew 3:17) is the divine recognition of the stock planted by the right hand.
16: "They have burned it with fire, they have cut it down; may they perish at the rebuke of your face!" — the vine cut down and burned. This images Christ's death: the true vine is cut down at Calvary, burned in the fire of divine wrath (Galatians 3:13: "cursed is everyone who is hung on a tree"). But the cut vine rises — the burned branch is not consumed but resurrected; the rebuke of God's face falls on Christ so that the divine face may shine on his people (refrain, v19).
17: "Let your hand be on the man of your right hand, the son of man whom you have made strong for yourself" — one of the most direct Messianic moments in the Psalter. "Ben-adam" (son of man) seated at the right hand of power, strengthened by God himself. Daniel 7:13 sees "one like a son of man" coming with the clouds to receive all authority. Jesus explicitly takes this title as his own self-designation (Matthew 8:20; Psalms 26:64: "you will see the Son of Man seated at the right hand of Power"). The man at YHWH's right hand of Psalms 80:17 is the ascended Christ (Acts 2:33; Hebrews 1:13).
18: "Then we shall not turn back from you; give us life, and we will call upon your name!" — the vow of faithfulness and the cry for life (hayyenu). Christ's resurrection answers this cry perfectly: it is the giving of life that makes calling on the name possible. "Everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved" (Romans 10:13, quoting Joel 2:32) — the life-cry of Psalms 80:18 is answered in the resurrection and the outpouring of the Spirit at Pentecost.
19: The final refrain in its fullest form: "Restore us, O YHWH God of hosts; make your face shine, so that we may be saved!" — the most complete divine name combined with the prayer for the shining face. Second Corinthians Psalms 4:6: the face of Jesus Christ is the shining face the psalm seeks. The escalating divine name through the psalm's three refrains traces the journey from lament to the full light of God's glory in Christ.
